  • Pontus 刘景源

    Pontus 刘景源

    2mo
    • Report this post
    Pontus 刘景源 shared this
    We recently got featured with Roots on one of UK's largest show This Morning as part of the growing conversation around healthier technology. Most apps don鈥檛 have users, they have hostages. Every swipe, every notification, and the weird moments when you open an app without knowing why.. None of this is accidental by the way. Most products quietly choose addiction because it's easier, makes more money, and it's the industry's default playbook. The pattern is familiar, especially with content feeds: Infinite scroll 鈫?No stopping cues 鈫?Constant cheap dopamine 鈫? You feel overstimulated and drained It's now 2026 and people are starting to push back for real. A recent global trends report highlights the rise of tech-free zones, quiet parks, and digital detox experiences. Not because all tech is bad and that we should refuse technology altogether, but because so much is designed without boundaries and it's gone too far. There鈥檚 a name for the alternative called "Guardian Tech" which very much is our design philosophy at Roots 1. Technology built to protect people, not extract from them 2. To support clarity over compulsion 3. To put people back in control Most design for dopamine hits and to spend more time. We design for clarity and want you to spend your time better. Experiences that people crave now isn鈥檛 louder, more digital, or stuffed with tokens 鈥?it鈥檚 calmer, more intentional and human. And this is why we're building Roots. Check out Roots in the comment below and give it a try to reduce your doomscrolling

  • Pontus 刘景源

    Pontus 刘景源

    12mo
    • Report this post
    Pontus 刘景源 shared this
    Hot Take: Formula 1 motion design is a mess. Oops, there I said it. More motion isn鈥檛 always the answer. Sometimes, it鈥檚 the problem. I took a look at the current grid formation graphics 鈥?and what I saw was a storm of effects. Elements flying in from all directions. Others completely static when they have every reason to move. Labels scattered, some rotated, some barely readable. And the worst part? A lot of it happened at once, with no clear hierarchy or logic. It looks exciting. But it doesn鈥檛 communicate. Motion design isn鈥檛 just about movement. It鈥檚 about meaning. 鈥?Here鈥檚 what I explored in this 30-minute redesign: 馃實 Motion that mirrors reality Instead of every driver entering at once, they animate in with subtle delays 鈥? just like their actual starting gaps. Each row drops in from the top, honoring how the grid is introduced: back to front. 馃幆 Focused attention, not visual clutter Rather than splitting up the grid into two completely different areas and an odd vertical "Starting grid" label in the middle(!?), we now have a clear title up top and the entire grid together below. The key here is also that the focus shifts as you go through the grid by highlighting each row as they take the stage. 馃彿锔?Smarter labeling, stronger hierarchy Team names sit right beneath the driver鈥檚 name鈥攚here people actually look. Would you rather know that he drives for McLaren or that his number is 4? Thought so. No sideways labels. No visual placements forcing us to do gymnastics at home to read it. 馃挕 Motion that serves meaning No spin for the sake of spinning. No splashes of pixelated waves coming in from both sides as if you were in an Avengers movie's war scene. Every transition and timing has the job of adding clarity and connection to the experience. There鈥檚 obviously so much more that could be explored and refined here 鈥?but that鈥檚 for another time. Curious to hear your thoughts馃憞 And where else have you seen motion that adds chaos instead of clarity? #motiondesign #formula1 #animation #uxdesign #productdesign
    public_profile__posts

    Here's a quick highlights video of last week's Livestream where I got hosted by Chris Do and The Futur. I also shared the case study of MasterClass and mindset when it comes to innovation lead by design and animation. In the end, I also shared a demo in After Effects of delightful animations. This was truly a pleasure and I'm excited about what's next. As I said multiple times. Your customers don鈥檛 care about your profit margins. They care about great experiences, period.   • Pontus 刘景源 reacted on this
    • Report this post
    Pontus 刘景源 reacted on this
    Suff Syed

    Suff Syed

    1mo
    Pontus 刘景源 reacted on this
    鉂?Notion launched Custom Agents today. I think this is the wrong direction. The idea is that you build custom agents for different tasks. Research agents. Writing agents. Project management agents. It sounds powerful. But think about what this actually asks of people. An individual now has to decide which agent to use, when. Every single time they have to do this, it's decision fatigue. A team of fifty people might create a hundreds of agents which makes this a governing nightmare from an IT perspective. That doesn't scale. There is a better model. Skills, not Agents. Every user should speak to ONE SINGLE ASSISTANT. That assistant should figure out which agents to orchestrate and what skills to deploy. Whether that's ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, or Notion AI. A single interface, a single relationship. When the user needs something done, the assistant draws from a repository of skills to handle the task. Sub-agents may spin up behind the scenes, but none of that complexity should ever be exposed to the person asking for help. The difference is who owns the complexity. Skills are managed by the assistant. Sub-agents are managed by the Assistant. The Assistant is managed by the user. The most design-forward companies in the industry keep making the same mistake. Shipping engineering abstractions without abstracting away the complexity. If AI is going to work for everyone, the complexity has to move down, not out. One assistant. Many skills. Zero overhead for the human in the chair. Ivan Zhao Akshay Kothari #notion #startups #ai #artificialintelligence https://lnkd.in/d22H3NNK
    Custom Agents are here! (CEO Message)
    Custom Agents are here! (CEO Message)

    Suff Syed

    Suff Syed

    1mo
    Pontus 刘景源 reacted on this
    馃Ж Perplexity has no moat. Figma has no moat. Cursor has no moat. Some of the best and most exciting companies of our time are slow dancing in a burning room. 馃寪 Perplexity's organic downloads have dropped greater than 80% as their marketing budgets have died down. Google AI Overviews now reaches over 1 billion users a month. Perplexity's bet was that search would be disrupted before Google could adapt. Google adapted, quickly. You can't out-distribute the company that owns the address bar. 鈫栵笍 Cursor hit $100M ARR faster than any company in history. Impressive, until you realize every dollar of inference they spend funds Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google. The same companies shipping Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini. Cursor is paying rent to landlords who are building better and cheaper apartments on the same high street. 馃帹 Figma has lost the plot. Their latest launch of Code to Canvas is a cry for help to validate the old ways of working. Nobody is going to work like that anymore. If AI keeps compressing the designer role, the TAM itself shrinks. More importantly, they've brought nothing worthy of justifying their existence in the last five years of the AI boom. The future designers aren't going to be pushing pixels on a canvas. They will be speaking their ideas into code and the models are only going to get more capable. Also, Figma Make is going to be exponentially more expensive than Cursor credits given its multi-modal nature. The pattern across all three: squeezed between foundation model providers integrating down and distribution giants integrating up. The burning room isn't the product. It's the architecture of the market these companies are in. #AI #artificialintelligence #startups

Recommendations received

  • Trey Nielsen

    鈥淲hen I co-founded Versus, I discovered Pontus' work and was instantly captivated. His designs were out of this world, but our project timelines didn鈥檛 align. At that point, I made it my mission to collaborate with him someday. After years of staying connected and envisioning a partnership, we finally joined forces to create a groundbreaking fan experience app for the motorsports industry鈥攁 project that showcased his unparalleled genius. Pontus is, without a doubt, the most extraordinary designer I鈥檝e ever worked with. I call him the Michael Jordan of designers, a personal analogy that reflects his unmatched talent and transformative impact. His portfolio is a masterclass in excellence, featuring iconic work for industry titans like Masterclass, Google, Avicii, and Netflix. These collaborations prove his ability to deliver world-class results for the biggest brands on the planet. Yet, Pontus is far more than a virtuoso of UX/UI and VFX, where he relentlessly pushes creative boundaries. He鈥檚 an inspiring human and a born leader who elevates every project. Pontus steps up, galvanizes teams, and propels ideas forward with unmatched vision and drive. Partnering with him guarantees not just the most stunning designs you鈥檒l ever see, but a dynamic leader who sparks momentum and delivers results. Pontus is my all-time favorite digital product professional. If you want to catapult your product and user experience to new heights, there鈥檚 simply no one better. He鈥檚 a game-changer, and working with him is an opportunity to create something truly extraordinary.鈥?

  • Mayur N. Vador

    鈥淚 recently had the privilege of bringing Pontus onto our team, and his impact has been nothing short of transformative. Pontus excels in pushing the boundaries of product design, seamlessly blending motion, 3D, and coding to craft innovative and memorable experiences. His positive attitude and collaborative spirit have also made him an invaluable asset to our team environment. For those seeking to revolutionize their product design, I cannot recommend Pontus enough.     So many brands think they鈥檙e choosing a 鈥渃heaper鈥? platform. Until they start adding up the developer hours. The plugins. The support tickets. The missed conversions. The lost time. And suddenly, that 鈥渓ow-cost鈥?choice? Not so low-cost anymore. That鈥檚 why I care more about total cost of ownership (TCO) than flashy price tags or features. Shopify recently commissioned an independent study comparing its TCO to other major platforms. Here鈥檚 the findings: 馃 Up to 36% lower TCO 馃殌 41% faster to launch 馃洅 Best-in-class checkout (Shop Pay boosts conversion by up to 50%) At Rainy City, we work with brands doing 7, 8 figures. And when the stakes are that high, inefficiencies add up fast. Choosing a platform isn鈥檛 just some tech decision. It鈥檚 one that impacts how fast you grow, how much you spend, and how hard your team has to work to keep things moving.     Mode Lab 鈥?18K followers

    Most people build prompt templates backwards. Use this prompt template instead.. Let Claude run discovery with you first. Not by asking it to "write me a prompt for X" (that rarely lands where you want it to). Have Claude act as your prompt engineer, asking you the questions that surface what you actually need. 饾棯饾椀饾棶饾榿 饾榿饾椀饾椂饾榾 饾椆饾椉饾椉饾椄饾榾 饾椆饾椂饾椄饾棽: I built a meta-prompt that turns Claude into a strategic thinking partner for prompt creation. 鉂?Instead of: "Write me a prompt to qualify leads" 鉁?You get: Claude asking you targeted questions about who's using it, what constraints exist, what good output looks like, and what failure modes to avoid. Then it architects the right prompt pattern: 鈻讹笍 single-shot 鈻讹笍 multi-shot 鈻讹笍 chain-of-thought 鈻讹笍 or hybrid 饾棯饾椀饾槅 饾榿饾椀饾椂饾榾 饾椇饾棶饾榿饾榿饾棽饾椏饾榾 饾棾饾椉饾椏 饾榾饾椉饾椆饾椉 饾棾饾椉饾槀饾椈饾棻饾棽饾椏饾榾: You're building agents and skills right now. But not every task needs a full Claude Skill. Sometimes you need a reusable prompt for common work: lead qualification, content briefs, proposal generation, meeting prep. The problem? Most prompts under deliver because they're built on assumptions instead of discovery. Let's fix that. I shared the full meta-prompt in my community. It includes: 鈥?The complete prompt (copy-paste ready) 鈥?Usage instructions for end users 鈥?Troubleshooting guide You'll get production-ready prompts every time. Not just for this, but as a repeatable process for every reusable prompt you need going forward. 饾棜饾椏饾棶饾棷 饾椂饾榿 饾椀饾棽饾椏饾棽: https://lnkd.in/gdbAATKt 鈾伙笍 Repost to help your network build smarter systems. 馃挕 Follow Ronnie Parsons for leverage-building insights. 馃敂 Turn on notifications to catch new implementations.

    Why Most Design Startups Are Not Ready for GenAI The design world is undergoing a quiet disruption. And most design agencies鈥攅specially in UI/UX, pitch decks, static websites, and social media鈥攁ren鈥檛 ready for what鈥檚 coming. Here鈥檚 what we鈥檙e observing: 鈥?Clients demand more quantity鈥攅specially in social content 鈥? Turnaround time expectations are shrinking 鈥?鈥淕ood enough鈥?design can now be AI-generated in seconds 鈥?   • Shahid Miah 馃

    Wavespace - UI/UX Design鈥?鈥?10K followers

    The right tools = better design decisions. Here鈥檚 my 5-part toolkit for building usable, lovable products. After working with 500+ SaaS teams and startup founders... I realized most design problems aren鈥檛 creativity-related. They鈥檙e process-related. So I built a tool-based workflow to fix it end-to-end.馃憞 1. Prototyping You don鈥檛 need perfect screens to test ideas. You need working flows. Use: 鈫?FigJam for quick sketches 鈫?Framer for interactive mockups 鈫?ProtoPie for smart transitions Great design starts with rough ideas, tested early. 2. Surveys Stop assuming what users want. Ask. Use: 鈫?Typeform for engaging surveys 鈫?Google Forms for quick feedback 鈫?Qualtrics for deep insights Good products start with good questions. 3. AI Design Assistants AI doesn鈥檛 replace designers, It helps them think faster. Use: 鈫?ChatGPT to structure flows 鈫?Uizard to turn sketches into screens 鈫?Khroma for AI-driven color palettes Think of AI as your co-pilot, not your competition. 4. Usability Testing You鈥檙e not the user. So, stop designing like you are. Use: 鈫?Maze to test quickly 鈫?UserTesting to observe behavior 鈫?Hotjar to track attention Real feedback is better than 100 assumptions. 5. A/B Testing Design is iteration, not a one-shot deal. Use: 鈫?VWO or Optimizely to compare versions 鈫?Convert to test page-level performance 鈫?Unbounce for landing experiments Let data guide your next move, not guesses. This is how we design smarter. - Use tools that work together. - Focus on insights, not opinions. - Build, test, learn, repeat. If you're a founder or product owner, This is your cheat code to ship a better UX. 馃捑Save this post.  鈾伙笍Send it to your team. And upgrade your design process.

  • Denise Miqueli

    Pratt Institute 鈥?984 followers

    Hot take for designers experimenting with AI + legacy systems: You don鈥檛 have to pick one AI tool and hope it integrates perfectly with your design archive. If you can get to the code, you can make it work. I鈥檝e been testing different AI platforms for strategy and prototyping. What I realized: You can use any AI tool you鈥檙e comfortable with to think, prototype, and generate UI 鈥?as long as it outputs code. Then: 1. Copy the generated code 2. Paste it into Figma Make 3. Let Figma rebuild it using your UI/style library 4. Edit, refine, and share inside your real system That means: 鈥?Strategy can live anywhere 鈥?Prototyping can move fast 鈥?Your design system still governs the final output AI becomes a thinking partner. Figma stays the system of record. For teams sitting on years of legacy designs and wondering how to bridge that with AI experimentation 鈥?this is a practical path. #DesignSystems #AIforDesign #Figma #ProductDesign #DesignOps

  • Sam Di Mauro

    Ordnance Survey 鈥?1K followers

    How I designed a rental home logo plate for my mom's rental in ~20 minutes with AI (link to ChatGPT thread below). Link to thread: https://lnkd.in/e6pFmxR2 Here's how I've done it: 1. (1 min) I dug on Google Images for some inspo. Landed with the official town crest, which I've pasted into the thread. (Link to the town crest for reference: https://lnkd.in/exHs-nqn) 2. (1 min) Prompted with some context of what I needed to do, plain and simple: I wanna transform this traditional town logo into a logo for an Airbnb house. It should represent a castle and the Faraglioni di Acitrezza, generate a friendly, minimalist and elegant logo. 3. (5 min)The AI came back with a generated image; it already felt closer to what I wanted to achieve, but it wasn't good enough. I've tried feeding a reference from another rental logo. It improved the output slightly, still not what I wanted. 4. (3 min) I try asking the AI export to an SVG file so I can continue in Figma with vector lines, but the export was completely broken. So I take a screenshot, I use union and boolean operations to create my logo. I already have solved the main problem of getting the inspiration and ideation; now I just need to refine and polish on Figma. 5. I spend 5-10 minutes adding the copy, refining the typography and choosing colours. My design is ready. Main learnings: 1. AI chats are not a perfect solution for refined designs. But it helped me ideate faster by simplifying the town crest using my prompt (friendly, minimalist, elegant). Use it to generate initial ideas based on what you want. 2. You need to understand when it's the right time to move to manual. I could have spent 30 more minutes trying to refine my logo on ChatGPT, but it felt like I wasn't getting anywhere. If you know the AI can't be pushed further, it's time to move to manual design tools. It will be quicker, and the quality of the output will be higher. 4. Because the AI generated a potential logo in no time, it made me quickly realise the Faraglioni (the piramidal rocks it generated initially) weren't fitting in the logo; it felt too crowded, and they looked like weird triangles. That allowed me to adjust the creative direction and focus on the castle and the flag. Again, helped me be faster in the refinement stage. 5. I needed something quick. I picked Helvetica Neue, which is the most basic font, quickly picked an earthy tone based on yellows and added the information needed for the plate. This is not the process I'd aim for if looking at developing a brand identity, for example. But for the job, I think the outcome was pretty solid and helped me come to a good enough solution, faster. Hope this is useful!

Learn to curate. 鈫扜reat output starts with great input 鈥?and clear goals. Stay strategic. 鈫?Design isn鈥檛 just what it looks like. It鈥檚 what it means. What are your thoughts?

  • Iqbal Ahmed

    Niofix 鈥?4K followers

    Charged $15K for a 2-week web redesign. No discounts. No long explanations. No awkward negotiations. And no 鈥?this wasn鈥檛 a US-only client. The biggest mistake designers make with global pricing is trying to look 鈥渁ffordable.鈥?That mindset quietly kills your positioning. I learned this the hard way. Early on, I priced based on: 鈥?Country 鈥?Currency 鈥?Client size It felt logical. It also trained clients to question my value. Here鈥檚 the shift that changed everything: I stopped pricing where the client is. I started pricing what the decision costs them. A homepage isn鈥檛 a page. It鈥檚 a revenue lever. A redesign isn鈥檛 visual work. It鈥檚 risk reduction. So my pricing model became simple: 鈥?One outcome category 鈥?Three clear tiers 鈥? Same logic globally No 鈥渞egional rates.鈥?No silent discounts. No apologies. What changes by tier: 鈥?Speed of delivery 鈥?Depth of validation 鈥?Level of ownership What never changes: 鈥?Strategic responsibility 鈥?UX accountability 鈥?Business impact That鈥檚 what clients pay for. The image breaks down: 鈥?How I frame tiers without feature dumping 鈥?Why higher prices reduce friction 鈥?How global clients actually evaluate UX spend 鈥?Where most designers underprice without realizing it If you鈥檙e undercharging globally, it鈥檚 rarely a market problem. It鈥檚 a positioning problem. Price the decision. Not the geography. #UXDesign #PricingStrategy #WebDesign #FreelanceUX

    Tech Stack for Product Designers in 2025 Like it or not, AI is here. With it, design discipline is changing. Designers are moving from slow-paced specialists producing static design to fast-paced generalists shiping usable code. From handoff to hands-on. From Figma only to frontend ready. It is important to upskill and adopt modern tech stack: 1. Figma Still core tool. Exceptional visual design skills and ability to build design systems are a must. 2. GPT (Claude, Grok, etc.) Create anything PRDs, UX copy, user flows, naming, and code snippets. 3. Cursor Designers can now write, test, and deploy working UI. 4. Base44 A vibe-code tool for quick prototypes and early concept validation. AI shift makes designers 10x faster, generating outputs that once took a full dev team. Yet this shift is not about tools. It is about autonomy, willingess to adapt and having core design skills (visual craft, user understanding and taste). P.S. It鈥檚 not about how fast you can build, but what and how well you can build it.

    Construction Technology Studio 鈥?608 followers

    Inspired by Chris Nager's post, I set out to create a custom MCP (Model Context Protocol) setup, one that could bridge CTS鈥檚 design system directly into Figma. I started by exporting our design system as a .json file using Studio Token in Figma, then spun up a local server with help from ChatGPT to break down the steps. From there, I used Windsurf to reference the system and generate a dynamic table that could be edited using just a prompt. It鈥檚 definitely not as advanced as Chris鈥檚 version, but seeing it work in action was a thrill, and a real step toward making prompt-to-production feel less like a buzzword and more like a workflow. Huge thanks to Chris for sharing his work and pushing this kind of innovation forward.   • Johan Adda

    Stobo 鈥?6K followers

    We Need Human-AI Design Principles now... Imagine you have to design and code Claude's front page, Midjourney's, or ChatGPT's. How do you design interactions for intent-based outcomes, for generative AI? Seriously, that's not an easy task. When I was a kid, I discovered the command line, DOS, to interact with a computer. Then we got these beautiful Graphical User Interfaces, and I became a software designer. Having designed for most platforms, from the web, phones, tablets, and most recently for spatial computing I can tell you that designing for generative AI is truly the first new UI interaction paradigm in 60 years (Nielsen's quote). My job was kind of easy. I had to read most guidelines that seasoned designers built upon their decades of experience. My favorite one is Apple's Human Interface Guidelines from 1987, a piece of art and also very helpful. That was decades of advice, rules, and guidance in the form of papers or online guidelines. Now, if you're like me and seek guidelines for human-AI interaction, well, as we know, it's a new paradigm, meaning there are very few guidelines available. The main issue in this new paradigm is that users write what they want, not how it should be produced. If you keep writing the same prompt, any generative AI will produce infinite variations of artefacts (I'm looking at you, Midjourney :). In contrast, if I write a command in my terminal, it will always produce the same output. So we're designing for generative variability (Weiz et al. 2023), a kind of algorithmic experience, with complete loss of user control and transparency, raising more problems than it solves: privacy, ethics, copyright, accessibility. You name them. I'm designing a conversational UI for our product at the moment, and I've already spotted the issues that any of our users will face: 鈽?blank page syndrome: what should I write first? 鈽?the tooltips below the chat box: what should I recommend? 鈽?context awareness: How/Where did the AI find this answer? 鈽?walls of text: a hard time retrieving information through infinite scrolling To be honest, I don't have answers yet. I'm trying to absorb as many research papers as I can find, but when I'm facing this chat UI design, I feel we're missing proper Design Principles. They will come, I'm not worried. So far, my focus for being able to ship our product is on users' mental models: 鉃?helping them understand our AI system's behaviour (the variability) 鉃?bringing more explanations of hidden AI features 鉃? teaching the AI about the user I will share more findings as I progress. I'm pretty sure there are a lot of people here who feel the same frustrations. Comment if you have any insights or advice. Time to build these principles.

No overhead, no long retainers. 鈿?Faster than traditional agencies Full-service shops are built for branding campaigns鈥攏ot fast-moving decks.
